Evidence for dark matter in galaxy rotation curves requires a non-baryonic particle component.
Modified Newtonian Dynamics (MOND) explains rotation curves without the need for dark matter by changing the force law at low accelerations.
The Bullet Cluster provides direct evidence for dark matter as the gravitational potential is offset from the baryonic mass.
Modified gravity theories like TeVeS attempt to explain gravitational lensing and structure formation without cold dark matter.
Weakly Interacting Massive Particles (WIMPs) remain a leading candidate for dark matter detection in underground experiments.
Emergent gravity and relativistic MOND models are being tested against cosmic microwave background observations.
Small-scale challenges to the Cold Dark Matter (CDM) paradigm suggest the need for self-interacting dark matter or modified physics.
The missing satellite problem can be resolved by baryonic feedback or by altering the dark matter power spectrum.
Gravitational waves from neutron star mergers constrain the speed of gravity and rule out certain classes of modified gravity.
Axion-like particles and fuzzy dark matter offer alternatives to the standard WIMP scenario in the search for missing mass.
Large-scale structure simulations with dark matter agree remarkably well with galaxy surveys like SDSS.
Modified gravity must reproduce the successes of General Relativity in the solar system while explaining galactic dynamics.
Direct detection experiments for dark matter have yet to find a signal, increasing interest in modified gravity alternatives.
The Tully-Fisher relation is a natural consequence of MOND but requires fine-tuning in dark matter simulations.
Cosmological constant and dark energy are often linked with the dark matter problem in unified dark sector models.
